svg.text: This project is used as a way for me to test out how much I can use AI to learn and create
code. So to be clear, a lot of the code in this project is first generated using an AI model
or two, and then heavily edited after generation; I've been using Claude, Deepseek, and
Gemini. Additionally, the styling of this entire README.md has been generated. The wording and
structure before the section "CLAUDE AI'S BREAKDOWN OF CODE" is my own. Besides all of that, the work is my own.
This project is actually a tool for a bigger web related project I've been working on, but the
style management got so out of control in that project that I had to refactor it. The refactoring
ended up throwing me into a hole of wanting to test out some models to create some serious
automation when it comes to styling pages, and so this project was born.
Additionally, this is technically my first TypeScript and Bun project. I say "technically" since
the bigger project I'm working on is actually the first TypeScript and Bun project I've worked on,
but this is going public first since I think it could be useful to others. Also, if you see anything
wonky or could be improved then please feel free to submit an issue and I can resolve it —
I'd love to improve my TypeScript skills.
The style_manager.ts and style_manager.js files can be used to create styles and CSS selectors
from an object purposed as a template to build styles from. The code revolves around the Style
class and templates called Style Definitions. When using the Style class and a definition,
a multitude of things will be generated:
An object of stripped CSS selectors.
An object of functions that can be used to style a page or singular elements.
Container — holds selector objects (no selector of its own)
Can be invoked to call all of their children functions.
You cannot pass elements into them.
Can be invoked in 2 ways. Simply calling them without an argument will apply the style
to the function's associated selector, and inserting an element as an argument will apply
the styles of the function to that element instead.
Stripped selectors are present for HTML builders. This is to ensure that generated HTML pages
follow not only a style definition but also to make sure the HTML, TypeScript, JavaScript, and
CSS are all using the same variables for styling.
Lastly, style definitions can be infinitely nested. The functions used to break down a definition
Will maybe make an option down the road to generate style definitions off of a template?
Make it so that function calls aren't string key lookups and just make them flat calls?
Everything from this point forward was generated by Claude, so take it with a grain of salt. I looked
over it and noticed some inaccurate notes that I had to remove, plus there was a usage example for
tag functions that had a typo I fixed; I tested the functionality using the body tag to confirm that
the model was wrong and that the typo was in fact a typo. If there's any other issues, I'm not seeing
them. But I could have missed something, let me know through an issue if that's the case.
A TypeScript utility for defining, organizing, and applying CSS styles programmatically. Style takes
a structured definition object and generates CSS selectors, DOM-ready selector strings, and styling
functions — making it easy to manage multiple themes or styles across a page.
When you construct a Style instance with a definition object, it automatically produces:
— same selectors without leading character, ready for HTML attributes
— callable functions that apply styles to matching DOM elements
The definition object is a nested structure where each key's first character acts as a type prefix,
and the remaining characters become the selector name.
Leaf nodes (non-object values) inside a selector object are treated as CSS property/value pairs.
Creates a new Style instance. If no definition is provided, an empty style is created.
An object mapping selector keys to their CSS selector strings.
The same as selectors, but with the leading CSS character (., #, *) removed.
Useful for setting className, id, or other HTML attributes.
Object of generated styling functions, keyed as style_<selector_key>. Call without an argument
to query the DOM, or pass an element to style it directly. Parent functions call all children.
Applies all styles in the definition to the current page by querying the DOM and styling every
matching element. Primary method for style switching — swap a Style instance and call apply().
// Switch to dark mode on button click
// Apply a style to a newly created element
Selector keys use _ as a separator when nested (e.g. class cMenu inside container oLayout becomes layout_menu).
